IMO `vocab_params` should be dealing only with the **input** of vocabulary construction and sys-argument passed `serializatio_dir/vocabulary` should be the output where the vocabulary constructed will be dumped. And there should be only one common (across all commands) interpretation of `vocab_params` that decides how to construct vocabulary that will be used. 

Until current commit in this PR, this is made consistent for `make-vocab` and `train`. `vocab_params` is used for input and its construction semantics is decided by `Vocab.from_params`. And output is always in sys-arg passed serializatio_dir (in "vocabulary" dir). 

The behaviour for `dry-run` should also be exact same for avoiding confusions of what is going to be input and where will be the output.

Here is the updated `dry_run_from_params` (I only needed to comment unnecessary things).

```python
def dry_run_from_params(params: Params, serialization_dir: str) -> None:
    prepare_environment(params)

    vocab_params = params.pop("vocabulary", {})
    # vocab_dir = vocab_params.pop('directory_path', None)

    # if vocab_dir is not None:
    #     logger.info("Found a vocabulary.directory_path parameter in your config. "
    #                 "Also saving the vocab we create to that location.")
    #     os.makedirs(vocab_dir, exist_ok=True)

    os.makedirs(serialization_dir, exist_ok=True)

    all_datasets = datasets_from_params(params)
    datasets_for_vocab_creation = set(params.pop("datasets_for_vocab_creation", all_datasets))

    for dataset in datasets_for_vocab_creation:
        if dataset not in all_datasets:
            raise ConfigurationError(f"invalid 'dataset_for_vocab_creation' {dataset}")

    logger.info("Creating a vocabulary using %s data.", ", ".join(datasets_for_vocab_creation))

    instances = [instance for key, dataset in all_datasets.items()
                 for instance in dataset
                 if key in datasets_for_vocab_creation]

    # vocabulary = verbosely_create_vocabulary(vocab_params, instances)
    vocabulary = Vocabulary.from_params_verbosely(vocab_params, instances) # Yet to add!

    logger.info(f"writing the vocabulary to {serialization_dir}.")
    vocabulary.save_to_files(os.path.join(serialization_dir, "vocabulary"))
    # if vocab_dir is not None and os.path.exists(vocab_dir) and os.listdir(vocab_dir) is not None:
```

Having now put a little more thought into this, I think no. 2 (appending to the end of the header on the outbound request) is more important than I realized initially-- not as much on account of the `X-Forwarded-Proto` header as on the account of the complementary `X-Forwarded-For` header.  The reason for this is that in the use case I described above, the router overwriting that header instead of appending to it results in the back-end service having no notion whatsoever of the end-client IP.  Usually that information would be captured in the `X-Forwarded-For` header, but if it's overwritten outright on the second trip through the router, the IP found in that header will be the IP of the service proxy-- which is less useful information than the end-client IP.

Long story short... we _do_ / _should_ figure out the proper way (even if it involves Lua) of amending `X-Forwarded-*` headers instead of overwriting them.

I don't think this is a good idea, and in direct discussions with other maintainers they have agreed, for a couple of reasons.

To explain our thinking a little bit
- The API doesn't have underlying support for this
  This has a couple of implications, among them that we'd be breaking the 1-to-1 mapping between methods and API calls that we try to maintain.
- It will be hard to maintain long-term and for all supported platforms/endpoint types
  One of our biggest concerns is maintenance burden. We understand and respect the desire for more features, but we need to weigh that against the difficulty of testing and maintaining those features.
- You can already implement it yourself
  The fact that you've been able to produce this code sample is already a huge step forward (from the Dark Times before the SDK), and it's not too much more work for you to wrap this up in a helper function of your own design. That protects against our maintenance concerns, and can be tailored to your specific needs.

If we had a `globus-contrib` space, as some projects do, I'd suggest that you submit this there, but we aren't sure there's enough interest to merit one.

> decision about signHash() issue.

Yeah, signing is ugly right now. The process for hashing messages is not well standardized. It's baked into the hardware keys, so we can't have a single `signMessage` implemented by all the different signers as a drop-in replacement. They all do it a little differently.

So to support ledger signing, it should probably just be a custom method. `ledgerSignMessage()` is an option for the name. Alternatively, you could name it `defunctSignMessage()` (if it's the exact same hashing process as `geth`). It's [called the `defunct` style elsewhere in eth-account](http://eth-account.readthedocs.io/en/latest/eth_account.html#eth_account.messages.defunct_hash_message).

You can raise a `NotImplementedError` from `signHash` saying that the Ledger doesn't support signing opaque hashes.

I understand that we can take code from conda, adapt it and use it, under the terms of the license. 
I don't like this approach, particularly in this case. 
This would be code which is no longer in conda, not benefiting from updates, changes etc. diverging over time and prone to API changes; it seems a problematicpath for me to take.

I am dealing with a situation where I have to maintain installable packages across a range of package management tools.  I think this situation is one that many package maintainers face.

For example there is a fedora package for numpy
numpy-1.8.2-2.fc20.x86_64
, a debian package
python-numpy (tho my debian machine don't yet have access to 1.8)
and a conda vesion
, amongst others.

I want to minimise the amount of code I need to deliver these various package solutions for my libraries and minimise the risk that packages for different tools are built differently, despite version compatibility.

If I can get enough functionality from conda that I can use it as my tool for building an rpm, then I can have generic code that just builds an rpm from a conda package (whichever conda package I need), picking up all the requisite details from conda as it goes.  This is a really powerful tool for me,  and I believe for others.

The code in the PR is a key piece of delivering this objective, and is a small extension to the conda code base.  

I am very interested in exploring the building of deployable packages such as RPMs using conda.

I have also since indirectly solved this problem by adding functions to my `~/.bashrc` which let me switch between using miniconda and the system Python:

```bash
export CONDA_PATH=$HOME/miniconda3/bin
export BASE_PATH=$HOME/bin:$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH

# Macros for switching between conda and system Python
function condafy {
    export PATH=$CONDA_PATH:$BASE_PATH
}

function noconda {
    export PATH=$BASE_PATH
}

# Use conda by default
condafy
```

Hey Omar,

Thanks for posting the results of then scan. I appreciate the work that
went into doing this.

I looked over the vulnerabilities and I think they fall into three
categories:

1) Untrusted SSL cert- this was the only high that I saw. I could be wrong.
I don't think there is a good way around this one. All ISY's come with
certain that are not publicly signed. This means that the CA cert is
unavailable to the user. The user could replace the default ssl cert with
one they generate, and thus, have the CA cert, but I think that requires a
level of complexity that most people won't want to deal with. Having
publicly signed certs, in most instances, is a little arbitrary too as that
means your ISY would have to be accessible via the internet and you would
have to have a domain name. That certainly is too much to ask the general
user. I'm open to feedback on this one. Possibly an option to turn on SSL
checking would be good. In general though, your ISY shouldn't be accessible
via the internet which means that this library would only be used on your
home (i.e. Trusted) network. So I think the threat is low.

2) XML Parser - for this one, I would again say that this should only be
run on a trusted network where the chance of exploitation is minimal. If
this library runs on a trusted network, then the ISY is implicitly trusted,
and the XML source is then trusted. Minidom is only vulnerable to billion
laughs and quadratic blowup, making it one of the more secure built-in XML
parsers as of Python 3.6. I'm not sure the mitigated threat is worth the
inclusion of an extra dependency. Again, I'm open to suggestions. I would
be open to seeing a PR that used diffusedxml.

3) General Excepts - yeah, that's just lazy programming. This is one of the
first things I wrote before I started getting very particular about best
practices. I'm all on board for cleaning these up because it is the right
thing to do. The security threat is low on these ones, as noted in your
scan, but still.. it should be cleaned up.

@vdmitrief Thanks for the question and I'll do my best to answer you here. But FYI this is not the location for help on matters of elasticsearch. 

For future reference please take a look at our discuss forums: https://discuss.elastic.co/
or Stack overflow: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/elasticsearch
or use IRC: https://webchat.freenode.net/#elasticsearch

These resources are much better for getting help on issues relating to using Elasticsearch. 

Issues here in GitHub are related to the python client for elasticsearch. 

Now that, that's all out of the way to answer your question. I'd suggest first looking at sorting your results, and then reversing that sort. https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/6.3/search-request-sort.html

By sorting your results you guarantee they are always going to be in a certain order. Then with the reversal of that sort you can guarantee that the first item in the reverse is actually the last item of your query. 

I hope that helps

> For example if I check that subscription x has not accepted the marketplace terms can I accept the marketplace terms or prompt the user to accept the terms?

It depends on an individual application's context. If your app just delegates the current user (like lots of web applications), then you can prompt users to accept the term; if your app runs under its own credential (like lots of LOB application) with access to the subscription, then you can just accept the term before create a VMs for users

imo, it's not support important to run hooks on a straight revert -- usually the goal of a revert is to get rid of a bad change as quick as possible.

If you wanted to run hooks in a "revert" situation, you could do it like this:

```
$ git revert --no-commit HEAD && git commit --no-edit
Trim Trailing Whitespace.................................................Passed
Fix End of Files.........................................................Passed
autopep8 wrapper.....................................(no files to check)Skipped
Check docstring is first.............................(no files to check)Skipped
Check JSON...........................................(no files to check)Skipped
Check Yaml...........................................(no files to check)Skipped
Debug Statements (Python)............................(no files to check)Skipped
Tests should end in _test.py.........................(no files to check)Skipped
Fix requirements.txt.....................................................Passed
Flake8...............................................(no files to check)Skipped
Validate Pre-Commit Manifest.........................(no files to check)Skipped
Reorder python imports...............................(no files to check)Skipped
Add trailing commas..................................(no files to check)Skipped
Check hooks apply to the repository..................(no files to check)Skipped
Check for useless excludes...........................(no files to check)Skipped
[master 7a2c914] Revert "Revert "this is a mistake""
 1 file changed, 1 insertion(+)
```

Note that `--no-commit` sets the default commit message and then `--no-edit` will use that default message.  It equates to the same thing as `git revert HEAD`, but it runs the hooks.

```
hmmlearn is a set of algorithms for **unsupervised** learning and inference of
Hidden Markov Models. For supervised learning learning of HMMs and similar models
see `seqlearn <https://github.com/larsmans/seqlearn>`_.

Getting the latest code
=======================

To get the latest code using git, simply type::

    $ git clone git@github.com/hmmlearn/hmmlearn.git

Installing
==========

Make sure you have all the dependencies::

    $ pip install numpy scipy scikit-learn

and then install ``hmmlearn`` by running::

    $ python setup.py install

in the source code directory.

Running the test suite
======================

To run the test suite, you need ``nosetests`` and the ``coverage`` modules.
Run the test suite using::

    $ python setup.py build_ext --inplace && nosetests

from the root of the project.

Building the docs
=================

To build the docs you need to have the following packages installed::

    $ pip install Pillow matplotlib Sphinx numpydoc

Run the command::

    $ cd doc
    $ make html

The docs are built in the ``_build/html`` directory.

Making a source tarball
=======================

To create a source tarball, eg for packaging or distributing, run the
following command::

    $ python setup.py sdist

The tarball will be created in the ``dist`` directory.

Making a release and uploading it to PyPI
=========================================

This command is only run by project manager, to make a release, and
upload in to PyPI::
```

I'm still puzzled why you're talking about backwards compatibility. You're adding a new operation that wasn't possible before, so there's nothing for it to be backwards compatible with. It doesn't matter that the same URLs already work for GET requests; POST requests are entirely separate. If you don't want to pass the path in the URL, make a new endpoint like `/nbconvert-config/html` (for instance). Or replace the existing nbconvert post handler - I don't think anything uses that.

Backwards compatibility is what forces us to make awkward compromises. You have the liberty of designing a new API that doesn't have to be backwards compatible with anything, so design it to be beautiful and simple and consistent, not involving a load of duplicate information with a 'backwards compatible' label stuck on it.

Does problem (2) still happen with explicit calls to the garbage collector?

The loopy references (which make the reference-counting garbage collection not work and which require some kind of mark-and-sweep strategy) are probably from the fact that nodes refer to tapes and tapes refer to nodes. The tapes aren't really a necessary data structure in the sense that they amount to a topological sorting of the computation graph (which is already encoded by the nodes pointing to each other), plus maybe a bit of extra bookkeeping (`tape.complete`). We might want to get rid of the tapes entirely.

A possible quick fix to try (that I don't see mentioned above) is to make the tapes keep weak references to nodes (as in a [weak reference list](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/677978/weakref-list-in-python)).

Revisiting this issue with some numbers from using TravisCI on large tool repositories: If we want to continue to use automated testing like TravisCI with large multi-tool repositories, then we need to have some sort of selective testing based on the recent git history (ideally spot changes since the last test run, and test any potentially affected tool).

The devteam and IUC have a couple of large GitHub repositories containing dozens of individual Galaxy tools or tool collections, as do I:

* https://github.com/galaxyproject/tools-iuc
* https://github.com/galaxyproject/tools-devteam
* https://github.com/peterjc/pico_galaxy

Even the BLAST+ collection maps to several Tool Shed entries, and the main collection now has many tools in it:

* https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_blast

As previously noted, these big repositories have advantages like being easy for new tool developers to search for usage patterns.

As you know I've been using TravisCI to run continuous integration functional testing of my Galaxy tools, but this works better with shorter test runs.  Currently galaxy_blast takes about 30 minutes
per target (including about 1 minute downloading and setting things up), while pico_galaxy takes about 40 minutes (including a larger set of dependencies to download and setup):

* https://travis-ci.org/peterjc/galaxy_blast/builds
* https://travis-ci.org/peterjc/pico_galaxy/builds

It is possible to test multiple targets in parallel (eg Python 2.6 vs 2.7, and Galaxy dev vs master branch), but four targets per build seems greedy with these long run times.

As an experiment yesterday I split out the MIRA wrappers from pico_galaxy into their own repository - TravisCI testing of this takes about 8 minutes per target (about 2 minutes of which is downloading and setting up planemo, Galaxy, and three versions of MIRA):

https://github.com/peterjc/galaxy_mira
https://travis-ci.org/peterjc/galaxy_mira/builds

[As an aside I have tried using "planemo test" here but there are some teething issues, e.g.
https://github.com/galaxyproject/planemo/issues/291 ]

Purely in terms of more immediate feedback from TravisCI, splitting up larger repositories like pico_galaxy seems like a worthwhile effort.

**Note that while the ratio depends on the tools and tests, setting up planemo and the Galaxy instance is a small fraction of the run times in these examples.**

In the absence of incremental testing, it is tempting to split things up even more so that one Tool Shed repository maps to a single GitHub repository, at which point the setup overhead (downloading and installing planemo, galaxy, and the tool dependencies) may be much more noticeable.

Some more thoughts about this today, this time on the negative side: A module definition describes a tree of files. The import path is not a property of that tree, it's a property of the larger project. One way this could get confusing is if you recursively import a module defined somewhere else. If the import path is given inside that remote module definition, does that mean it should apply to your project too? (It won't.)

For the sake of clarity even if this is a repeat I am NOT talking about implicit code-level deps (e.g. from an `import`) and packaging-level deps (e.g. from a setup `install_requires`) which would both need to be there in the first place and are handled by Python and the setup/install machinery.

```
  --setup-only          only setup fixtures, do not execute tests.
  --setup-show          show setup of fixtures while executing tests.
  --setup-plan          show what fixtures and tests would be executed but
                        don't execute anything.
```

The certificate you're looking at is not a Let's Encrypt certificate. Let's Encrypt doesn't put any of the X.509 contact information such as `S`, `T`, `L`, and `OU` into our certificates, we weren't issuing certificates to the public early enough for one to expire in August 2015, we wouldn't issue a certificate for a non-FQDN like "share", and we have never permitted issuance of certificates for 1024-bit subject public keys. You must be looking at a certificate or a server that's somehow configured to use some other certificate, not one of ours.

Its not a bug. We can impute multiple features. Here are some scenarios. All of these share the same API at the moment and they all impute.

- Single row prediction on a dataset with multiple features and one is missing
- Single row prediction on a dataset with one feature and that one is missing
- Batch prediction on a dataset with a single feature in a single row missing
- Batch prediction on a dataset with an entire feature missing

The principle applied during imputing is a point estimate that minimizes the variance in the prediction. Its not a bug at all and is completely by design to accommodate a consistent API and behavior for all 4 cases above.

Hi Mark, 

Great to hear you like the library. 

I am going to separate the "MiSeq - the QScores are 100% which obviously isn't reflected in the data." into another issue. Please respond to this issue in this thread: https://github.com/Illumina/interop/issues/53

It would help to know when you are seeing 100%, during the run, end of run and such? Also, could you attach an example Q-metric Interop file with a RunInfo.xml to the newly created issue? It would really help to figure out the problem.

**NextSeq & MiSeq - the clusterPF gets written for both reads as soon as Read1 data is availabe. Is this normal?**

Yes. The PF is the same between read 1 and every read after.

**MiSeq to extract the current step and cycle**

This is available in the InterOp files under the following conditions:
1. It may be lagging behind the sequencer depending on when it is flushed to disk
2. The MiSeq workflow is different from most other sequencers. It will perform extraction on as many tiles as possible, before going back and basecalling.
3. If you are reading these files from a network drive, rather than on instrument, then there may be other delays depending on the run copy service.

SAV generally reports three state numbers:
- Last cycle extracted
- Last cycle basecalled
- Last cycle q-scored

For MiSeq, you will see the last cycle extracted may be much later than the last cycle base called. For most other sequences, all three will be less divergent. 

Right now, the summary logic provides the last cycle error rated. You can access that in either the lane summary or the run_summary  from the cycle_state method. The error_cycle_range is the only one currently populated. 

The start of the range is the latest cycle where all tiles have been error rated, where as the end of the range is latest cycle that any tile has been error rated. 

I created another issue https://github.com/Illumina/interop/issues/54 to populate the other cycle states. If you have further questions on this topic, please use this thread.

Just wanted to point out that `(+ (- 1) str.len)` means `str.len - 1`, while `(- 1 str.len)` means `1 - str.len`. So, the latter probably causes z3 to recognize the string can be at most 1 character long before that index becomes negative, and thus it can easily exhaust the cases; while the former would be a much harder problem as the proof would be over a much larger search space. That would explain the performance difference, as the problems are significantly different. Was that intentional in your encoding?

> Should we check and enforce that `lower < upper` and that they are both in `[0, 1)`?
And if so, should I raise an exception if this invariant is violated?

`lower < upper` -- yes, and raise a `ValueError` if not. Note that you can only check this if they are not symbolic variables. If `isinstance(lower < upper, theano.Variable)`, then silently skip the test in the constructor.
You could also allow `lower == upper` and fall back to not using any randomization in that case (can be merged with the `if deterministic` check in `get_output_for()`).
About the range: The formulation of the rectifier in Theano allows arbitrary slopes for the negative part, even negative slopes. So I think this shouldn't be constrained.

> I'm also not entirely sure how to best write `test_rrelu`. Looking at `test_prelu`, I'm thinking one way to do it would be to ensure all the negative slopes have been limited to within `x * lower` and `x * upper`, but that doesn't seem very satisfactory.

That's the best you can do, I guess. You could compute the slopes that were used for negative inputs and ensure they are in the correct range and not all the same (or, stricter, have a variance reasonably close to what's expected). In addition, you should have a test with `deterministic = True`. When adding a range check and exception to the constructor, you'll also need a short test for that, Coveralls is not forgiving us anything.

Since I cannot find a complete implementation of @f0k's idea, here is my try. Unlike @icemansina's implementation, my version do not normalize over batch axis (0th axis) by default (`axes='auto'`). I believe this is the intention of Ba, Kiros & Hinton (2016). But I'm new to deep nets so I could be missing something.:

```python
import lasagne


class LayerNormLayer(lasagne.layers.BatchNormLayer):

    def __init__(self, incoming, axes='auto', **kwargs):
        if axes != 'auto':
            kwargs['axes'] = axes

        super(LayerNormLayer, self).__init__(
            incoming,
            beta=None, gamma=None,
            **kwargs)

        if axes == 'auto':
            self.axes = tuple(range(1, len(self.input_shape)))

    def get_output_for(self, input,
                       batch_norm_use_averages=False,
                       batch_norm_update_averages=False,
                       **kwargs):
        return super(LayerNormLayer, self).get_output_for(
            input,
            batch_norm_use_averages=batch_norm_use_averages,
            batch_norm_update_averages=batch_norm_update_averages,
            **kwargs)


def layer_normalized_dense_layer(incoming, num_units,
                                 nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify,
                                 W=lasagne.init.Normal(std=1),
                                 b=lasagne.init.Constant(0.),
                                 **kwargs):
    assert num_units > 1
    layer = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(
        incoming, num_units, W=W, b=None,
        nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.linear,
        **kwargs)
    layer = LayerNormLayer(layer)
    layer = lasagne.layers.ScaleLayer(layer)
    layer = lasagne.layers.BiasLayer(layer, b=b)
    return lasagne.layers.NonlinearityLayer(layer, nonlinearity=nonlinearity)
```

The above code works only for non-recurrent layers so I also have another very "hacky" implementation which relies on the (possibly) implementation detail of Lasagne that `nonlinearity` property can be array-to-array function rather than a scalar function:

```python
import lasagne
import theano


class LayerNormalization(object):

    def __init__(self, num_units,
                 nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify,
                 b=lasagne.init.Constant(0.),
                 g=lasagne.init.Constant(1.),
                 eps=1e-5):
        self.num_units = num_units
        self.b = theano.shared(b.sample(num_units), name='layer_norm.b')
        self.g = theano.shared(g.sample(num_units), name='layer_norm.g')
        self.eps = eps
        self.nonlinearity = nonlinearity

    def normalizing_nonlinearity(self, x):
        mean = x.mean(-1, keepdims=True)
        sigma = theano.tensor.sqrt(x.var(-1, keepdims=True) + self.eps)
        b = self.b.reshape((1,) * (x.ndim - 1) + (-1,))
        g = self.g.reshape((1,) * (x.ndim - 1) + (-1,))
        return self.nonlinearity(g * (x - mean) / sigma + b)

    def register_to(self, layer):
        layer.add_param(self.b, (self.num_units,))
        layer.add_param(self.g, (self.num_units,))


class RecurrentNormalizingLayer(lasagne.layers.RecurrentLayer):

    def __init__(self, incoming, num_units,
                 W_hid_to_hid=lasagne.init.Normal(std=1),
                 b=lasagne.init.Uniform(0.1),
                 g=lasagne.init.Constant(1.),
                 hid_init=lasagne.init.Uniform(0.1),
                 nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.rectify,
                 eps=0.05,
                 **kwargs):
        self.layer_normalization = LayerNormalization(
            num_units, nonlinearity=nonlinearity, b=b, g=g, eps=eps)
        super(RecurrentNormalizingLayer, self).__init__(
            incoming, num_units,
            W_hid_to_hid=W_hid_to_hid,
            b=None,
            hid_init=hid_init,
            nonlinearity=self.layer_normalization.normalizing_nonlinearity,
            **kwargs)
        self.layer_normalization.register_to(self.hidden_to_hidden)
```

I guess the same idea can be used for LSTM and GRU.

Regarding benchmarking, I threw together this little bit of code, and it looks like the summing is somewhat faster than concatenation, at least for a very simple case run on my laptop CPU.  I'll try to run it on a GPU hopefully tomorrow.

```
from lasagne.layers import DenseLayer,NonlinearityOnlyLayer,ConcatLayer,ElemwiseSumLayer,InputLayer 
import theano.tensor as T
from numpy.random import RandomState
from timeit import timeit

def setup():
    m1 = rng.rand(1000, 1000)
    m2 = rng.rand(1000, 1000)

    m1_sym = T.matrix()
    m2_sym = T.matrix()
    in_layer1 = InputLayer((1000, 1000))
    in_layer2 = InputLayer((1000, 1000))
    input_dict = {in_layer1: m1_sym, in_layer2: m2_sym}
    
    concat_layer = ConcatLayer([in_layer1, in_layer2])
    output_concat = DenseLayer(concat_layer, 100)
    foo_concat = theano.function([m1_sym, m2_sym],
                                 output_concat.get_output(input_dict),
                                 allow_input_downcast=True)

    dense1 = DenseLayer(in_layer1, 100, nonlinearity=None)
    dense2 = DenseLayer(in_layer2, 100, nonlinearity=None)
    output_sum = NonlinearityOnlyLayer(ElemwiseSumLayer([dense1, dense2]))
    foo_sum = theano.function([m1_sym, m2_sym],
                              output_sum.get_output(input_dict),
                              allow_input_downcast=True)

    return foo_sum, foo_concat, m1, m2

print("concat:", timeit('foo_concat(m1, m2)', number=100,
                        setup="from __main__ import setup; foo_sum, foo_concat, m1, m2 = setup()"))
print("sum:", timeit('foo_sum(m1, m2)', number=100,
                     setup="from __main__ import setup; foo_sum, foo_concat, m1, m2 = setup()"))
```

Disregard. I figured out my problem. In my 'class ContactAdmin', the call was: 
resource_class = ['ContactResource']
When it should just have been: 
resource_class = ContactResource
I knew this from the documentation but thought maybe it was an error because my Django webserver was crashing. Then I realized it was my error, not the documentation (should have figured). My ContactResource class came AFTER my ContactAdmin class in my admin.py file. So basically, ContactAdmin was trying to call ContactResource class which had not been instantiated yet and that's what was causing my web server to crash with: 
    
resource_class = ContactResource
NameError: name 'ContactResource' is not defined

Why would it be slow and taxing? Using the xml API seems like a viable option. You'll have to download as many xmls as the number of channels in the subscription feed, get all the entries from all the xmls, then sort them according to the 'published' attribute using `datetime.strptime` as key function.

> If you allow adding a sub-path then you need to accept that sometimes it will have slashes in and sometimes it might have %-encoded slashes that are intended to be retained as such. And then when you pass that string on somewhere and it is decoded then any sub-paths with slashes in will be itemised separately.

The approach right now is to just raise an exception, which seems like it neatly short-circuits all these issues.

Is there a reason you left out ``swt2``, ``idwt2``?

> however, why you opted for such different definition ? even 1/pi^(1/2) ... is that correct or it's a typo 


The normalization used here is consistent with the definition in [Matlab](https://www.mathworks.com/help/wavelet/ug/wavelet-families-additional-discussion.html?searchHighlight=wavelet%20family&s_tid=doc_srchtitle) and their documentation references the following source:
`Teolis, A. (1998), Computational signal processing with wavelets, Birkhäuser.`

I accessed that text electronically via my university library and they state that this form makes the L1-norm of the wavelet equal to 1 (and that it's Fourier transform will have a peak value of 1).  A quick numerical check seems to verify this claim:

```python
import pywt
import numpy as np
wav = pywt.ContinuousWavelet('cmor')
psi, x = wav.wavefun()
dx = x[1]  - x[0]

L1 = np.trapz(np.abs(psi)) * dx  # L1-norm is the integral of the magnitude
print("Integral of magnitude = {}".format(L1))
```

I am not sure the reason one would use this normalization vs. others.  Unfortunately my expertise in the CWT is limited and the author of the CWT routines in PyWavelets hasn't been active here lately. I think in typical applications the absolute normalization shouldn't be too important? Isn't one typically looking for features in a spectrogram where a global scaling of the plots shouldn't matter?

Thanks everyone for the feedback so far!

Some thoughts so far. Perhaps the place to start is with a list of top-level subdirectories and a metadata file?

- Main content subdirectory: Seems like `warcs` is more commonly used, though `archives` may be more generic, as I suppose arcs and perhaps some other format can go there?. Any preferences on warcs/archives (or some other name)?

- `derivatives` seems to make sense as good catch all for various types of extracted content. Not sure if it makes sense to standardize beyond that yet. Great that it would work for your use case @ruebot 

- Should there be a top-level `logs` directory as it may mean different types of logs? Would having
`capture/logs` and `capture/viral` work for your use case @anjackson ?

- `access` and `entrypoints` may be pywb/replay software specific, though perhaps a way to extend these as well.

The list of possible directories might be:
```
- archives
- indexes
- derivatives
- capture
- access
- entrypoints
```

Regarding a basic manifest file: I like the Frictionless data form as its JSON based. Perhaps a yaml-based format could work `manifest.yaml`?  On the other hand, BagIt is more widely adopted, though not JSON based.. Any more thoughts about BagIt?

The manifest could also include which subdirectories are included, and could be used to generate other files, like dat.json for DAT.

Regarding version history: I agree with @anjackson that perhaps best to leave versioning to existing tools like dat/git. By "Support for multi-service workflows" @atomotic  I had in mind that one service could add WARCs and commit, and another service could check out and add derivatives, mostly thinking of using DAT and/or git.

It seems that having hashes is important, but perhaps should be optional? What should be 'required', at minimum, to have something in this format, just the subdirectory structure and a manifest file?
Thinking about DAT use case, where a partial sync should be possible, a user might retrieve only the manifest and one subdirectory.

I didn't fully understand what's going on here, and I haven't been following the changes to ddragon for the last couple weeks. Can you please give a bit more info on what's going on here? I'd just like to understand the issue before merging.

So the most minimal I see is this statement:

```sql
    CREATE TABLE reflection_default_value (
        col1 INTEGER NOT NULL,
        col2 INTEGER DEFAULT 5,
        PRIMARY KEY (col1)
    ) DISTSTYLE EVEN
```

Which errors because in the tests, it gets cleaned into this statement:


```sql
    CREATE TABLE reflection_default_value (
        col1 INTEGER NOT NULL,
        col2 INTEGER DEFAULT 5,
        CONSTRAINT reflection_default_value_pkey PRIMARY KEY (col1)
    ) DISTSTYLE EVEN
```

It looks like redshift is adding a new constraint %s_pkey on primary key. I don't know if it was doing that before but somehow the introspection is finding it after the table is created and it errors because of the mismatch.

We could probably update the tests to match the new behaviour. Redshift constraints, aside from NOT NULL, have always been informational only so I don't think this changes anything... but if someone has contacted support about it to get clarifications that's something we should probably wait for.

@amitsaha the errors field in the response is implemented following the `GraphQL` spec:
https://facebook.github.io/graphql/#sec-Errors

This field (`errors`) will be added if any exception occurs while processing the query (validation or field retrieving).

The errors will be written in the response as a field in the json, however you can always wrap the response checking if any errors and changing the HTTP status code accordingly.

Hi @act65 and @PFCM,

This is a cool idea. Unfortunately, we don't plan on adding this sort of functionality, but I'd be happy to review and accept a contribution if you decide to contribute.

Regarding implementation, it will be a decent-sized change. You will need to encode the sound url/data in [PointMetadata](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/84b7dd0b2e02aacbbf9f21100c049f4d3d2d48aa/tensorboard/plugins/projector/vz_projector/data.ts#L27). I recommend using a reserved key for this data (e.g. `__AUDIO__`).

Then you need to register a custom `hoverListener` [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/84b7dd0b2e02aacbbf9f21100c049f4d3d2d48aa/tensorboard/plugins/projector/vz_projector/vz-projector.ts#L445) if metadata of type `__AUDIO__` is found in the data. Then on hover you need to render/play the audio using html.

Feel free to re-open this issue when when/if decide on sending us a contribution.

OpenThread intentionally delegates full control of managing IPv6 addresses for prefixes obtained via the Network Data to the application.  We follows this model for a few reasons:

1. We are targeting devices with limited resources.  Ultimately, the application dictates the IPv6 addressing requirements (i.e. what IPv6 prefixes are of interest and how those IPv6 addresses should be configured).
2. While RFC 7217 should be used, there are cases where other IPv6 address formation schemes may be used.  Thread explicitly allows manual configuration of IPv6 addresses as well.
3. In scenarios where OpenThread is operating in an NCP configuration, this model allows the host-side responsible for managing the host's IPv6 interface (e.g. `wpantund`) to maintain control of the IPv6 addresses assigned to the interface.

`python-escpos` does not currently have the ability to define custom code pages.

You're correct that the commands for this are heavyweight. To truly switch a printer to the modified HP-Roman 8 character set, you need a few things-

- A column-format representation of the entire font, probably 24x12 font.
- Upload characters in the `0x20` to `0x7F` range using user-defined character set `ESC &` command
- Upload characters in the `0x80` to `0xFF` range using functions under the `GS ( E` command
- Switch the printer to use the user-defined character set and code page with `ESC %`, and the custom code page 255 with `ESC t`.

These commands are all available on the TM-T20 (I don't think TS20 is a real printer, is that a typo?). If you set up the code page, the printer would then understand any of the (printable) Roman 8 characters.

So you would have to write a lot of code, which is the bad news. Good news is that if you can figure out how to map your text to Unicode, you have two other options.

## Magic encoder

I typed out the contents of this code page in UTF-8 using the Wikipedia link in your post[1]

```
 !"#$%&'()*+,-./
0123456789:;<=>?
@ABCDEFGHIJKLMNO
PQRSTUVWXYZ[\]^_
`abcdefghijklmno
pqrstuvwxyz{|}~▒
◄▲▼►╝╗╔╚╣╩╦╠═║╬♦
↑▀▄↓┘┐┌└┤┴┬├─│┼█
 ÀÂÈÊËÎÏ´`ˆ¨˜ÙÛ₤
¯Ýý˚ÇçÑñ¡¿¤£¥§ƒ¢
âêôûáéóúàèòùäëöü
ÅîØÆåíøæÄìÖÜÉïßÔ
ÁÃãÐðÍÌÓÒÕõŠšÚŸÿ
Þþ·µ¶¾-¼½ªº«■»±
```
When I send this text file through `escpos-php`[2] on the default profile[3], it looks like this:

![2018-03-hp-roman8-text](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2080552/36637643-a5997be4-1a34-11e8-96a0-21b69327ccb7.png)

The magic encoder for `python-escpos` uses the same algorithm, so try just sending text with special characters from the printer and see how you go. The "??" characters are used when the characters aren't available.

## Unifont

I'm currently prototyping a new encoder[4] which dynamically swaps Unifont[5] characters through the user-defined character set area, allowing you to print basically any glyph.

```
cat modified-roman-8.txt | php escpos-snippets/unifont-bitmap-font/unifont-example.php
```

![2018-03-hp-roman8](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2080552/36637524-c6ac1d58-1a31-11e8-9f53-2a030840920c.png)

This is not yet available in any of the big open source drivers, but if you are interested in implementing this for `python-escpos`, then let me know- I'm trying to come up with a way to properly support CJK scripts at the moment, so we may be able to collaborate.

(PS: Apologies for using PHP in these examples!)

[1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP_Roman#Modified_HP_Roman-8
[2] https://github.com/mike42/escpos-php
[3] http://mike42.me/escpos-printer-db/#profiles/default
[4] https://github.com/mike42/escpos-snippets/tree/master/unifont-bitmap-font
[5] http://unifoundry.com/unifont.html

```python
import tensorflow as tf
import tflearn
import random

X = [[random.random(),random.random()] for x in range(1000)]

g1 = tf.Graph()
g2 = tf.Graph()

with g1.as_default():
  n = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None,2])
  n = tflearn.fully_connected(n, 2)
  n = tflearn.regression(n)
  m = tflearn.DNN(n)

with g2.as_default():
  n2 = tflearn.input_data(shape=[None,2])
  n2 = tflearn.fully_connected(n2, 2)
  n2 = tflearn.regression(n2)
  m2 = tflearn.DNN(n2)

Y = [[x[0],x[0]] for x in X]

m.fit(X, Y, n_epoch = 20)
print(m.predict([[0.9,0.1]]))
with g1.as_default():

print(m2.predict([[0.9,0.1]]))
with g2.as_default():
```

The extra `_d` is set [here](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/36919ea6955a790c2c61a2cdc44f7796027b5532/tools/FindPythonLibsNew.cmake#L139), but the comment right above does state `# TODO: All the nuances of CPython debug builds have not been dealt with/tested`. The `FindPythonLibsNew.cmake` file dates back to before Python started using ABI tags for extension modules, which should have solved this issue. The `PYTHON_MODULE_EXTENSION` in CMake is determined as the result of running:
```python
from distutils import sysconfig
sysconfig.get_config_var('SO')
```
I just checked on Linux and the release and debug builds of Python 3.5 return `.cpython-35m.so` and `.cpython-35dm.so` respectively. Python 2.7 returns `.so` and `_d.so`. This also works as expected on Windows. So it would seem that manually adding `_d` is not needed since the string returned by `sysconfig.get_config_var('SO')` already has it.

@wjakob Ok I'm in the process rewriting the initial flags branch (given all the recent big changes like ctors rework; plus many features in the original PR have been already implemented, like NumPy C API access). One question that emerged is this: how do we encode the `readonly` / `writeable`? I've added `buffer_info.readonly` attribute, so now that needs support on the `array` / `array_t` side.

One problem is that `ExtraFlags` are used for two completely different purposes:

1. construction from pointer - all ctors get essentially get forwarded to `array::array()`
2. conversion of an object - `array_t<...>::ensure()`

Here are a few points:

1. When constructing/converting arrays, many flags act as *requirements* only, this affects whether a copy will be made (only if the array is required to be writeable, but a passed array is not). If you *don't* request writeability, it doesn't mean that array will *not* be writeable -- it may be writeable or it may be not, who knows.
2. Having template-level parameters for run-time properties is generally a bit strange. You can always do `array_proxy(m_ptr)->flags |= NPY_WRITEABLE` (or do it from Python). This means that if `array::writeable` was a flag that you could pass to `ExtraFlags`, then `array_t<T, array::writeable>` would not in general be guaranteed to be writeable since it's a run-time property. 
3. Sometimes you would want to enforce readonly property on a newly created or an existing array -- i.e., set the flag manually on the array. Logically, it would be passed to ctor and not as a template argument (although the current ctors already have a ton of arguments, including ones with default values...).
4. However, sometimes you would want to either ensure the array you receive as an argument to your function is either (a) writeable so you can mutate it, or maybe (b) relax writeability requirement (NB: != readonly), so that NumPy doesn't accidentally make a copy where it shouldn't. Obviously, this has to reside outside of ctors (either a template argument; or what?).

(One other idea I had was that if a certain thing (like writeability *requirement* for arrays, or alignedness) is only required for converting incoming arguments, they could be into "extra" arguments pack that we use for return value policies etc; but that would probably be a mess so I'm not suggesting that).

I also just realized (correct me if I'm wrong) that `ExtraFlags` are not used at all for array creation, i.e. the instance created by `array_t<T, flags>::array_t()` does not depend on `flags` at all since it gets forwarded blindly to `array::array()`, discarding all flags. Is that intended?

Hm, on data() returning void* by default, this causes a slight annoyance - one almost always uses the return type with strides, adding in byte offsets. As such, it is best if it is unsigned char /uint8_t *.

Otherwise you will commonly write lines like reinterpret_cast<T *>(reinterpret_cast<uint8_t *>(arr.data()) + arr.strides[0]) (along with constness mutations).

Most image processing toolkits use uint8 for this reason, it makes it much more convenient to compute the byte level offsets.

In this case, it appears you installed only python-certbot-apache.  This would pull in certbot as a leaf dependency, and cause it to be suggested for removal when you remove python-certbot-apache.

You can validate this by running `apt-mark showauto *certbot*`.  If it returns certbot in that list, then it was automatically installed as a dependency (probably by `apt-get install python-certbot-apache`), rather than as a direct installation.

You can tell apt that you want to keep certbot around after removing python-certbot-apache by running: `apt-mark manual certbot`.  Then it should be safe to remove python-certbot-apache.

1) Running with Docker:

> You can't install a cert that you get via Docker because the plugins can't reach into the Docker container to get it.

Actually, all of Certbot, including the plugins, are running inside of Docker. The problem is the plugins can't reach outside of the container. So, for example, if you're running an Apache webserver and then use Certbot, Certbot is unable to reach outside of Docker to modify Apache.

> We should add a sentence that starts with "If you use Docker, you'll have to..." and then explains what the docker run command below will do. What does it do?

Using the long `sudo docker run ...` command given in our documentation, Certbot will obtain a certificate and place it in the directories under `/etc/letsencrypt` on your system. The [Where are my certificates?](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#where-certs) linked in to in our Docker documentation further explains the layout of this directory. After running Certbot, the user will have to manually install their new certificates (because as explained above, Certbot is unable to do so while running inside of Docker).

> Here, we're also recommending certbot-auto. Should we be recommending OS package instructions instead? In what cases should we recommend certbot-auto?

In my opinion, the main use cases for using Docker with Certbot are:

1. Power users who only want to use Certbot to obtain a certificate.
2. Sysadmins who like the repeatability Docker containers provide.
3. Users who want to minimize the amount of changes Certbot makes to their system. This could be due to security concerns or people wanting more control over the process.

In general, these are advanced users and I think all/most of them going in will know they want to user Docker. For the average user, OS packages and `certbot-auto` should be preferred and OS packages should be preferred over `certbot-auto`. People should be using `certbot-auto` if there aren't OS packages available on their system.

2) Operating System Packages:

> I understand that on Debian, Ubuntu, and other Linux distros, the package exists in one of the default repositories and you can just apt-get or yum install or whatever. On FreeBSD and OpenBSD, does the /usr/ports/security/py-letsencrypt directory exist right out of the box?

Kind of. On BSD systems, the package instructions are similar to the `apt-get` or `yum` Linux users are familiar with. Someone makes a precompiled package that other users can download and easily install on their system.

The port system on the other hand, allows users to compile and install programs themselves using the source code. By default, `/usr/ports` is empty, however, the user uses a set of command similar to `apt-get update` which will fill `/usr/ports` with all the files they need, including `/usr/ports/security/py-letsencrypt`.

To directly answer your question, no, the files are not there out of the box. With that said, creating those files is done through basic, generic commands that a user runs to install anything from ports and for a BSD user, it really is as basic as running `apt-get update`. With this in mind, I personally don't feel  we need to specify this, but I could be convinced otherwise. For more information, see [packages and ports](https://www.freebsd.org/doc/en/articles/linux-users/software.html) from FreeBSD's official documentation.

3) Proposed change based on your comments:

> Change "Unless you have very specific requirements" to "If a package does not exist for your operating system".

I think that's a step in the right direction, however, the rest of that paragraph goes on to talk about how `certbot-auto` is the "best", "fastest", and "most reliable" method. In general, I think the paragraph should say something along the lines of:

Unless you have very specific requirements, we kindly suggest that you use the Certbot packages provided by your package manager. If such packages are not available, we recommend using `certbot-auto`, which will automate the process of installing our software on your system.

See: https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/cc24b4e40af5841c8dfddeecd9bde7d1acce62e8/tests/boulder-integration.sh#L456 This is something I want to change with this PR though and with the change, your understanding here is correct.

I'm not saddened by us not running the latest Certbot integration tests with an older version of Certbot. I think trying to get that to work is an unnecessary headache with little to no benefit.

To be a little pedantic again, the `py27-*-oldest` bit does determine what unit tests are run. This is the conventional purpose of setting the `TOXENV` environment variable. You're correct though that this PR makes them also control which integration tests are run.

> I think that specifying boulder environment should not secretly under the hood determine which tests to actually run
I think that everything that's done should be done clearly
so, perhaps, splitting into BOULDER_INTEGRATION and NGINX_BOULDER_INTEGRATION
because those are now both additional tests that we might want to run

We're currently using the value of `BOULDER_INTEGRATION` to determine whether ACMEv1 or ACMEv2 is used. This affects boulder setup and some of our Certbot integration tests are hidden behind a check that `BOULDER_INTEGRATION` is set to ACMEv2.

I also would like to avoid relying on a lot of environment variables which makes it harder to see what tests are running in Travis' UI. We currently only have 2 integration tests, but we will hopefully have integration tests for Apache and the DNS plugins in the near future.

If you don't like `TOXENV` controlling what integration tests are run, what if we had three environment variables:

* `TOXENV` controls which environment is used by `tox`.
* `ACME_VERSION` which is set to 1 or 2.
* `INTEGRATION_TEST` which is a comma/space delimited list of the packages to run integration tests on. We could also have a value like "all" for convenience and readability.

I don't want to require people set all of these variables when running integration tests locally so I'd want to set some defaults here, but this gives each variable a single purpose.

The tagtool's way of looping is by complete startup-shutdown cycle for the whole stack including device driver. In driver startup the very first chip communication with evaluated response is the get_firmware_version() command and there seems to be no response data. Why that happens is difficult to say without debug logs (maybe even with).

In general I would recommend for your type of application to avoid always opening and closing the driver but just loop over card detection. The examples/sense.py script follows this approach and is maybe already sufficient for just getting the card ID.

They have put together this controlled environment to have version control of the software tools (and its dependencies) and provide automated builds of any flavor of container (e.g. docker, singularity, rkt, other in the future). This is why I tried sos as a first candidate. We have used it in our multiple Omics integration scheme recently.
Next would be sos-notebook. On the longer run multi-package containers can be build via this [website](http://biocontainers.pro/multi-package-containers/). 
I see the potential and would like to evaluate the effort to use this bioconda/biocontainers framework in the future by exploring some use cases. Other than sos, the MOFA Omics tool is on the radar but there we first asked to include the code base in pypi and bioconductor.
Hope this was useful to clarify our thinking.

That is not necessarily the case because `_n` is paired with `_input` and is a list matching each element in `_input`. The problem here is that `_input` can be used for `_input[0]` when there is only one `sos_target`  but `_n` cannot be used for `_n[0]` in the same case.

The technical problem here is that we know what to do for list of strings (`paths`) but `n` can be of arbitrary type and `_n` can be tricky to deal with in some case. For example, if `n` is a nested list, say,

```
n = [[1,2], [3,4]]
```
In the current implementation
```
_n = [[1,2]]
_n = [[1,2], [3,4]]
```
has known behavior, but if `_n` is
```
_n = sos_list ([[1,2]])
_n = sos_list([[1,2], [3,4]])
```
Then
```
_n[0]
```
can be
```
[1,2]
```
in the second case because `_n` has more than one element and `_n[0]` refers to the first element, and can be
```
1
```
in the first case because `_n` has only one element and `_n[0]` refers to the first element of the only element.

This was why I restricted the default to first element behavior only to `paths` (and then `sos_targets`).

That's understandable. The trio code appears more tied into the GLOBAL_RUN_CONTEXT than curio's fairly generic code, so it would be harder to make trio's socket class (for example) accept all the things it needs as arguments.

Note that the issue isn't quite the same thing as making a generic interface to multiple asynchronous IO libraries, like multio. It's more about allowing a modular approach to constructing new asynchronous IO libraries, by allowing components to be reused. The fact that trio has different low-level semantics from other libraries doesn't matter - one would only use components from trio if one was building a library which had similar low-level semantics.

> Can you give more details on the problems you ran into with lack of fairness in uasyncio?

uasyncio initially used heapq module for scheduling queue, with the usual trick of letting few tasks be scheduled for the same time unit : https://docs.python.org/3/library/heapq.html#priority-queue-implementation-notes (store entries as 3-element list including the priority, an entry count, and the task.). Having 3-element entry instead of 2-element meant spending 50% more memory on it (with MicroPython heaps counted in kilobytes), and was one of the big motivations to rewrite it to dedicated "timeq" modules.

But then turned out, that because heap data structure doesn't provide "stable sort" order, then if certain number of tasks are scheduled for the same time unit, then some of them don't get scheduled fair. If the other tasks also called `uasyncio.sleep(0)`, then they got rescheduled at the *same time unit* (millisecond for uasyncio) and *before* the "unlucky" task(s). As an example, if we scheduled tasks 1, 2, 3, 4, then only tasks 1, 2, 3 had a chance to run, and 4 was always dangling at the end of queue and barely had a chance to run.

There're of course few conditions which lead to that, namely: a) finite precision of time scheduling; b) not really true randomized, but rather biased, "sorting". But the point is still: having a deterministic scheduling is *boon*, which helps to fight even unfortunate circumstances like above. So, in uasyncio, if tasks were scheduled in the order 1, 2, 3, 4, they will run in that order (and if they just yield to the loop each (not timed wait, no I/O), they will keep running in that order.

@njsmith Really interesting ideas! I specially like the "full decomposed design" route as a way to think about it all and explore possibilities. I also agree in generat that it's somehow necessary to "decompose" and "expand" de possibilities before trying to "simplify" the "user experience" or trying to simplify the API too soon (I mean, to soon in the design process. I see it as a kind of "too-early optimization" in the design process, with all that means!).

As further help to reason about what "elements" and "responsabilities" are needed and why, I think it would be useful to have a set of emblematic use cases or examples making different uses of the "available" (fully decomposed) APIs. You have already given some examples above, but it would be useful to explore some other use cases trying to make use of the new "available" elements. Some ideas in the form of questions could be:
* Given a `CancelScope`, what if I needed to ask it which task (or which "cancel binding") triggered the cancellation of the scope?
* What if a "cancel binging" could store some more "data" (or meta-data) about the moment it was created (which may not be the same moment that the `CancelScope` was created)? Or about the task "it was created for"?
* What if I wanted to be able to shield a `CancelScope` from "some kind of "cancel bindings" and not others"?? I think this last sentence makes no sense at all if we see the "cancel bindings" (or "cancel tokens") as simple references to the "cancel scope" but... what if we see them as some kind of "access rights" to cancel the `CancelScope` ("cancel rights" may be a better term here). Could shielding then be expanded to be some kind of `chmod` for cancel scopes? Does it help to think in new directions?

```python
functools.partial(<bound method SectionProxy.get of <Section: a>>, _impl=functools.partial(<bound method RawConfigParser._get_conv of <configparser.ConfigParser object at 0x7f37f424c860>>, conv=<class 'int'>))
>>>
```

Last, the cache for the swagger spec is not race-condition safe.  Either we should save it with some checksum, or we should save it to a tempfile, and then os.rename it to its final location (os.rename is atomic while file writes are not).

@bsteffensmeier  

- Why are there multiple pom.xml files? I thought most projects only need 1.
I think the multi-level pom makes sense is because it might be more convenient to trigger a build command from the top level just like the "python setup.py install" as opposed to making the user switch to one level lower just for the java artefact.  The number of pom.xml files is proportional to the number of modules in a given project.  This allows for building inside the lower level modules only if required ( with some caveats) for quick dev/test cycles.
- What does jep-parent mean? For the artifactId think this is the name of the jar in which case I would prefer just jep. I'm not sure what it means in the name of the iml file though?
The reason I went in for jep-parent is because of the parent and child module relationship. Note that the jep-parent is only a shell and does not produce any "jar" artefact. It exists so that we can control common property definitions at the top level where the main build command would be triggered and all of the child modules inherit those properties. 
The actual artifact that is the jar is named "jep-java-dependencies" as Jep is a union of many constructs and we are clearly calling out that this artefact is for the java components. 
- Are the test classes included in the jars? Ideally the test classes would be omitted from the distributed jars.

Turns out registration was fine, but picking the target frame was messed up because we subtract out y and x means at every iteration. With parallelism, the means were computed per batch, and the reference image ended up blurred. The fix was to just shift the mean image at each iteration by the mean of the respective Y and X offsets.

We'd like to establish a stable "v1" in the next few weeks. ONNX will continue to evolve but we agree there needs to be a checkpoint to target.

I'd like to propose that we use semver semantics - update major version for breaking changes, update minor version for backwards compatible changes. We should apply this principle to the version field already in the IR.

There are several options for ops:
1) version them as a set with the IR
2) version them as a set separate from IR
3) version them as several sets/groups separate from IR
4) version each op separately

1 may be problematic since ops will evolve faster than the IR.
4 may get unwieldy.

Hey, so finally got some time to reply to these:
1) SymbolNodes and TokenNodes:
Good question. I thought on this a bit and you might be right; but some earlier tests led me to believe not. The SymbolNodes are added to PackedNodes and disambiguated again through the set() implementation of children on PackedNodes. The memoization deals with SymbolNodes that are repeated in the same parse cycle, but doesn't deal with a new SymbolNode being added in a later parse cycle. However, the later parse cycle SHOULD have a higher 'start' value and shouldn't be duplicated anyway, so I need to find some more tests. I don't have time to deep-dive this right this second, but I'm still working on this long-term as I still need yet more performance out of this anyway. Another round of tuning on the SPPF is definitely on the horizon. Long term I want to remove SymbolNode and TokenNodes completely and replace them with tuple's and lark.Token's anyway to avoids additional object creation so that will change the implementation a bit. But I want to do that in a way that keeps readability.
2) Item & node: Yes, I think you're probably right. The Item doesn't need a Node constructor; I think it's a remnant from a previous life :-)
3) Item.rule: Still need to dig on this one.

I need to check out the work you did on the PR but I hopefully have some time later this week, so I'll pull and rebase all my parsers on this. :-)
